title: Capstone: File Room Handoff
summary: Use the Linux terminal to turn a messy event workspace into a clean handoff package with sorted files, a backup copy, removed clutter, and a final ready marker.

sketchBlocks.title: Start by inspecting the workspace
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
In a real handoff task, the safest first step is to **look before you change anything**. In the Linux terminal, `pwd` shows where you are, and `ls` shows what is in the current folder.

A simple inspection flow looks like this:

```bash
pwd
ls
cd event_drop
ls
```

Step by step:

- `pwd` prints your current location.
- `ls` lists the files and folders where you are now.
- `cd event_drop` moves into the folder you need to organize.
- `ls` again lets you see the messy contents before you sort them.

This matters because many terminal commands change files immediately. If you inspect first, you are less likely to move or remove the wrong item.

sketchBlocks.title: Build a clear folder structure
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Once you know what is in the workspace, create a structure that makes the handoff easy for the next person to understand. `mkdir` creates folders, and `touch` can create an empty marker file.

Example:

```bash
mkdir handoff
cd handoff
mkdir notes photos archive
touch READY.txt
```

Line by line:

- `mkdir handoff` creates the main folder for the final delivery.
- `cd handoff` moves into it.
- `mkdir notes photos archive` creates three subfolders in one command.
- `touch READY.txt` creates an empty file that can act as a status marker.

A good folder structure reduces confusion. Instead of leaving files scattered around, you group them by purpose.

sketchBlocks.title: Move, copy, and rename files carefully
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Terminal file work often uses three commands together:

- `mv` to move or rename
- `cp` to make a copy
- `touch` to create a new file when needed

Worked example:

```bash
mv notes.txt handoff/notes/event_notes.txt
cp handoff/notes/event_notes.txt handoff/archive/event_notes_backup.txt
mv photo-list.txt handoff/photos/photo_manifest.txt
```

What each line does:

- The first `mv` both moves the file and renames it.
- `cp` creates a backup copy in another folder.
- The second `mv` places another file in the right folder with a clearer name.

This is useful in a handoff because the final workspace should be organized, readable, and safe if someone needs a backup copy later.

sketchBlocks.title: Remove clutter without damaging important files
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Use `rm` only when you are sure a file is temporary or no longer needed. In beginner terminal work, it is best to remove a specific file by name instead of using broad patterns.

Example:

```bash
ls
rm temp.txt
ls
```

Why this is safe:

- The first `ls` confirms the file is really there.
- `rm temp.txt` removes only that one file.
- The second `ls` verifies that the clutter is gone.

This habit helps you avoid deleting the wrong thing. In a capstone cleanup task, removing one known temporary file is much safer than using more advanced destructive commands.

sketchBlocks.title: Verify the final handoff package
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A finished terminal task is not complete until you verify the result. You can use `ls`, `cat`, and `wc` to confirm that the workspace matches the goal.

Example:

```bash
ls handoff
ls handoff/notes
cat handoff/READY.txt
wc -w handoff/notes/event_notes.txt
```

How to read this:

- `ls handoff` checks the top-level handoff folders and files.
- `ls handoff/notes` confirms the notes file is in the right place.
- `cat handoff/READY.txt` shows the marker file contents.
- `wc -w` counts words in a file, which is a quick way to confirm a note file is not empty.

Verification is the final quality check. It helps you leave a workspace that another person can trust and use right away.

quizDraft.title: Create the park survey request folder
quizDraft.prompt: You are helping a park survey team prepare a terminal map. Create final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests and add final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt so the team has a concrete request log to hand off.
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Use the terminal to make one small workspace change at a time, then inspect the result in the file explorer.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.starterCode: # Use the terminal for this Linux project task.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
mkdir -p final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests
printf 'North trail request log\n' > final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt
ls final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey
quizDraft.instructions: You are helping a park survey team prepare a terminal map. Create final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests and add final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt so the team has a concrete request log to hand off.
